meta.certificationName: AWS Certified Generative AI Developer – Professional
meta.title: AWS-GAP-4: Bedrock Retrieval, Safety, and Operational Design

questions.stem: A chat application needs a request format designed for multi-turn conversations with Bedrock models. Which API is the best starting point?

options.explanation: The Converse API is designed for conversational inference requests and responses.
InvokeAgent is for Bedrock Agents orchestration rather than the base conversational API.
CreateKnowledgeBase provisions retrieval infrastructure, not chat inference.
PutMetricData sends metrics to CloudWatch.

questions.stem: A workload needs a straightforward single model invocation without conversational state handling. Which API is most appropriate?

options.explanation: InvokeModel is the direct single-request inference API.
ConverseStream is for streaming conversational responses.
CreateAgent provisions an agent resource rather than invoking a model directly.
Evaluation-job deletion is unrelated to inference.

questions.stem: Why might a developer choose `ConverseStream` instead of `Converse`?

options.text: To stream tokens or chunks back as the model generates them
options.explanation: The streaming variant is used when incremental response delivery is useful.

options.text: To force asynchronous batch scoring only
options.explanation: Batch scoring is a different feature.
Streaming does not inherently disable history.

options.text: To create a Knowledge Base automatically
options.explanation: It does not provision retrieval resources.

questions.stem: When selecting a Bedrock model family, what should the engineer compare first?

options.text: Model capabilities, constraints, and workload fit
options.explanation: Model choice should start with capability and workload fit.
Alphabetical order is irrelevant.
Identifier length is irrelevant.
Weather has nothing to do with model selection.

questions.stem: What is the primary role of an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base?

options.text: Provide managed retrieval-augmented generation over connected data sources
options.explanation: Knowledge Bases are for managed retrieval over enterprise data in RAG workflows.

options.text: Replace IAM policies for all AI apps
options.explanation: IAM is still required separately.

options.text: Train a new foundation model from scratch
options.explanation: They do not train base models from scratch.
Monitoring dashboards are a separate service concern.

questions.stem: A RAG system needs both semantic relevance and keyword matching over documents. Which search mode is most aligned with that need?

options.explanation: Hybrid search combines semantic and lexical signals for retrieval.
Traffic split is a deployment behavior.
Provisioned throughput is a capacity model.
Denied topics are a guardrail setting.

questions.stem: What is an action group in Amazon Bedrock Agents used for?

options.text: Connecting the agent to callable business actions such as Lambda-backed operations
options.explanation: Action groups expose operations the agent can invoke to complete tasks.
They do not store model weights.
CloudWatch alarms are separate.
Prompt cache keys are unrelated.

questions.stem: A team wants a supervisor pattern in which one agent routes tasks to specialized capabilities. Which Bedrock feature area is most relevant?

options.text: Agent orchestration with Amazon Bedrock Agents
options.explanation: Bedrock Agents are the feature family for agent orchestration patterns and tool/action execution.
Throughput reservation is a capacity concern.
Guardrails are safety controls.
Batch manifests are for offline jobs.

questions.stem: What can Amazon Bedrock Guardrails enforce?

options.text: Content filters, denied topics, and sensitive information protections
options.explanation: Guardrails include safety filters such as denied topics and PII-related controls.
EC2 sizing is unrelated.
Routing tables are networking concerns.
S3 lifecycle is storage management.

questions.stem: A healthcare chatbot must avoid exposing personally identifiable information in outputs. Which Bedrock control is the best fit?

options.text: A guardrail with sensitive-information handling such as PII redaction
options.explanation: Guardrails provide sensitive-information protections for outputs.
Provisioned throughput affects capacity, not PII policy.
A batch manifest controls offline processing inputs.
Knowledge Base sync refreshes retrieval content.

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects AWS guidance on customer prompts and outputs in Amazon Bedrock?

options.text: You should review the documented data protection behavior and retention controls for the service path you use
options.explanation: AWS documents service-specific data protection behavior and customers should align architecture to those controls.

options.text: Bedrock always publishes every prompt to other tenants
options.explanation: AWS does not document Bedrock this way.

options.text: Data protection is irrelevant for generative AI
options.explanation: Data protection remains central to generative AI design.

options.text: IAM alone replaces all data handling decisions
options.explanation: IAM is necessary but not sufficient for overall data handling.

questions.stem: When is provisioned throughput usually the right choice in Amazon Bedrock?

options.text: When you need predictable capacity and latency for sustained production demand
options.explanation: Provisioned throughput reserves model capacity for predictable production traffic patterns.

options.text: When you only run one tiny experiment once a quarter
options.explanation: Tiny infrequent experiments usually do not need reserved capacity.

options.text: When you want to avoid creating IAM roles
options.explanation: IAM is still required.

options.text: When you need a Knowledge Base data source
options.explanation: Knowledge Base sources are a different feature.

questions.stem: Why might batch inference be preferable to online invocation?

options.text: It is suited to large asynchronous processing jobs where immediate per-request responses are unnecessary
options.explanation: Batch inference is designed for high-volume asynchronous processing.

options.text: It is the only way to use Bedrock models
options.explanation: Online invocation is also supported.

options.text: It is required for every RAG application
options.explanation: RAG can use online inference too.

options.text: It disables all output safety controls
options.explanation: Safety controls are not universally disabled by using batch jobs.

questions.stem: What is a main benefit of prompt management in Bedrock?

options.text: Versioning and reusing prompts more consistently across applications
options.explanation: Prompt management helps teams store, version, and reuse prompts consistently.

options.text: Increasing GPU memory on the foundation model
options.explanation: Prompt management does not change model hardware.
IAM remains separate.

options.text: Automatically retraining the model on every prompt
options.explanation: It does not retrain the model automatically.

questions.stem: A team wants to reduce cost and latency in a generative app before changing models. Which action is often a good first step?

options.text: Trim unnecessary tokens and repeated prompt content
options.explanation: Token reduction is a direct and common way to improve cost and latency.

options.text: Increase randomness to create shorter outputs
options.explanation: Randomness is not a reliable cost optimization strategy.
Disabling evaluation weakens quality control.

options.text: Route every request through all tools
options.explanation: Mandatory fan-out usually increases cost.

questions.stem: What does Amazon Bedrock model evaluation help with?

options.text: Assessing model quality with automated or human evaluation workflows
options.explanation: Model evaluation supports quality assessment using automated and human review approaches.
S3 replication is unrelated.
Subnet provisioning is networking.
IAM policies are separate infrastructure controls.

questions.stem: A team wants humans to compare two prompt variants for helpfulness and preference. Which Bedrock capability is most relevant?

options.text: Human evaluation within model evaluation workflows
options.explanation: Bedrock model evaluation supports human evaluation workflows for preference-style judgments.
Throughput affects capacity, not quality review.
Word filters are safety controls, not comparative evaluation methodology.
Knowledge Base sync refreshes retrieval data.

questions.stem: Why should a prompt change be regression tested before broad rollout?

options.text: Because quality can drift even when infrastructure remains unchanged
options.explanation: Prompt changes can materially alter quality, relevance, and safety outcomes, so regression testing is important.

options.text: Because Bedrock models cannot answer updated prompts
options.explanation: Bedrock models can answer updated prompts.

options.text: Because IAM policies become invalid after every prompt edit
options.explanation: IAM validity is unrelated.

options.text: Because Guardrails stop working after one deployment
options.explanation: Guardrails do not stop working due to a prompt edit.

questions.stem: Which TWO elements are core to a Bedrock RAG design? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: RAG depends on a retrievable corpus or index.

options.text: A model invocation path that can use retrieved context
options.explanation: The application also needs to pass retrieved context into a model invocation flow.
A dashboard theme is irrelevant to RAG behavior.
Placement groups are unrelated.

questions.stem: A team wants to block a specific category of disallowed content in every response, regardless of user phrasing. Which control is most direct?

options.text: A Bedrock Guardrails denied topic policy
options.explanation: Denied topics are a direct guardrail mechanism for disallowed categories.
Provisioned throughput is about capacity.
Version labels do not enforce output policy.
Scheduling does not enforce content rules.

questions.stem: A team only needs simple inference today but may later add tools and more structured conversations. Which starting approach is most reasonable?

options.text: Begin with the simplest API that meets the current need and add complexity as requirements justify it
options.explanation: AWS architectures should start with the smallest capable design and add complexity when requirements warrant it.

options.text: Implement every advanced Bedrock feature immediately
options.explanation: Premature complexity increases cost and risk.

options.text: Use only human evaluation and skip inference APIs
options.explanation: Evaluation does not replace inference.

options.text: Reserve throughput before any traffic exists
options.explanation: Unused reserved capacity is wasteful.
